lessons.moduleTitle: Lecture 1: Mendelian genetics in natural populations
lessons.moduleDescription: Allele and genotype frequencies, sequence diversity, Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium, and departures from random mating.
lessons.title: Allele and genotype frequencies from population samples
lessons.summary: Convert genotype counts into allele and genotype frequencies, verify internal consistency, and distinguish sample estimates from population parameters.
objectives: Calculate allele frequencies from diploid genotype counts
Check that genotype and allele frequencies sum correctly
sections: From genotype counts to frequencies

For a biallelic diploid locus with counts NAA, NAa and Naa, the sample size is N=NAA+NAa+Naa. Genotype frequencies are obtained by dividing each count by N. Each homozygote contributes two copies of its allele and each heterozygote contributes one copy of each allele, so allele frequencies are calculated from 2N sampled gene copies.
Internal checks before interpretation

The three genotype frequencies must sum to one, and p+q must equal one for a biallelic locus. These checks detect transcription or coding errors before biological interpretation. A frequency estimated from a sample is not the exact population frequency; its precision depends on sample size and sampling design.
Why population genetics starts with frequencies

Evolutionary models track the distribution of alleles or genotypes through time. Counts are therefore converted to frequencies before testing equilibrium, drift, migration, or selection. The same conversion is used in practical analyses after genotype filtering and sample assignment.
lessons.workedExample: In 100 individuals, 36 are AA, 48 are Aa and 16 are aa. p=(72+48)/200=0.60 and q=0.40. The observed genotype frequencies are 0.36, 0.48 and 0.16.
commonMistakes: Dividing allele counts by N instead of 2N
Using percentages and proportions in the same calculation
Treating the sample frequency as error-free
lessons.keyStatement: Allele frequency is obtained by counting allele copies, not individuals.
distractors: Allele frequency is calculated from phenotype labels only.
Every individual contributes one allele copy.
A sample frequency has no sampling uncertainty.
trueFacts: For a biallelic locus p+q=1.
A heterozygote contributes one copy of each allele.
Genotype frequencies must sum to one.
falseFacts: Allele counts in diploids are divided by N.
The rarer allele must always be q.
lessons.aiPrompt: A sample contains 52 AA, 36 Aa and 12 aa individuals. Calculate p and q, show two independent checks, and explain what the estimates do and do not tell you about the population.
lessons.sourceBasis: QBio305 Lecture 1 slides, exercises and practical material.

lessons.moduleTitle: Lecture 1: Mendelian genetics in natural populations
lessons.moduleDescription: Allele and genotype frequencies, sequence diversity, Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium, and departures from random mating.
lessons.title: Quantifying molecular diversity
lessons.summary: Calculate heterozygosity and nucleotide diversity and explain why different diversity estimators weight allele frequencies differently.
objectives: Interpret nucleotide diversity as average pairwise difference
Compare estimators based on frequencies and segregating sites
sections: Expected heterozygosity as a probability

Gene diversity is the probability that two randomly chosen gene copies are different. For two alleles it is 2pq; for multiple alleles it is one minus the sum of squared allele frequencies. Rare alleles contribute less than intermediate-frequency alleles.
Nucleotide diversity from aligned sequences

Nucleotide diversity, pi, is the mean number of pairwise nucleotide differences per site. It uses every pair of sampled sequences and is therefore sensitive to the frequencies of variants, not only to the number of segregating sites.
Segregating sites and the frequency spectrum

The number of segregating sites increases with sample size and is corrected through Watterson’s estimator. Comparing pi with an estimator based on segregating sites reveals whether variants are concentrated at low, intermediate, or high frequency, which later motivates Tajima’s D.
lessons.equation: H = 1 - Σ p_i²;  π = [Σ pairwise differences] / [C(n,2)L]
lessons.workedExample: At a biallelic locus with p=0.7 and q=0.3, H=2pq=0.42. For four 100-bp sequences with a total of 12 pairwise differences across six pairs, pi=12/(6×100)=0.02.
commonMistakes: Calling heterozygosity the observed fraction of heterozygous individuals in every context
Forgetting to divide pairwise differences by sequence length
Comparing raw S across samples of different size
lessons.keyStatement: Diversity statistics summarize different features of the same allele-frequency distribution.
distractors: Heterozygosity counts only homozygotes.
Nucleotide diversity ignores allele frequency.
Raw segregating-site counts are independent of sample size.
trueFacts: Expected heterozygosity is a probability.
Pi averages pairwise differences per site.
Rare variants contribute less to heterozygosity than intermediate variants.
falseFacts: H can exceed one.
Pi is calculated from one arbitrarily chosen pair.
lessons.aiPrompt: For allele frequencies 0.5, 0.3 and 0.2, calculate gene diversity. Then explain why adding a very rare fourth allele changes H less than adding an intermediate-frequency allele.

lessons.moduleTitle: Lecture 1: Mendelian genetics in natural populations
lessons.moduleDescription: Allele and genotype frequencies, sequence diversity, Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium, and departures from random mating.
lessons.title: Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium as a null model
lessons.summary: Derive expected genotype frequencies from allele frequencies and use deviations as a diagnostic rather than a causal explanation.
objectives: Compare observed and expected genotype counts
Identify processes that can generate deviations
sections: The mating-table derivation

If gametes carry A with probability p and a with probability q, random union of gametes gives AA with probability p², Aa with probability 2pq and aa with probability q². The expansion (p+q)²=1 provides the algebraic structure.
What equilibrium means

Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium predicts genotype frequencies after random mating under the stated assumptions. It does not imply that allele frequencies are unchanging under every evolutionary process, and a statistical deviation does not identify its cause.
Diagnosing departures

Heterozygote deficiency can arise from inbreeding, selfing, population mixture, null alleles, or genotyping error. Heterozygote excess can arise from disassortative mating or selection. Sampling and multiple testing must be considered before biological interpretation.
lessons.workedExample: With p=0.6, q=0.4 and N=100, expected counts are 36 AA, 48 Aa and 16 aa. Observed counts should be compared with these expectations, not with equal genotype frequencies.
commonMistakes: Using observed heterozygosity as 2pq without checking context
Interpreting a deviation as proof of selection
Forgetting that population mixture can create a Wahlund effect
lessons.keyStatement: HWE translates allele frequencies into a genotype-frequency null expectation.
distractors: HWE predicts equal genotype frequencies.
HWE identifies the cause of every deviation.
The heterozygote frequency is pq.
trueFacts: Expected heterozygosity is 2pq for two alleles.
Observed and expected counts can differ by sampling.
Population mixture can reduce observed heterozygosity relative to pooled HWE expectation.
falseFacts: A HWE test can prove adaptation.
p²+2pq+q² can differ from one when p+q=1.
lessons.aiPrompt: A population has 50 AA, 20 Aa and 30 aa. Calculate p and q, obtain HWE expected counts, describe the direction of deviation, and list at least three compatible causes.

lessons.moduleDescription: Random sampling, fixation and loss, heterozygosity decay, and effective population size.
lessons.title: Drift as random sampling of gene copies
lessons.summary: Understand why allele-frequency change occurs without directional selection and derive its variance under binomial sampling.
objectives: Predict effects of population size and allele frequency
sections: Sampling from one generation to the next

Under neutral Wright–Fisher sampling, the next generation contains 2N gene copies drawn from the current allele pool. The expected allele frequency remains p, but individual realizations fluctuate around p.
Variance is largest at intermediate frequency
The variance of the next-generation allele frequency is p(1-p)/(2N). It is zero at p=0 or p=1, largest near p=0.5, and decreases as population size increases.
Replicate populations diverge

Independent populations beginning at the same frequency follow different trajectories. Their mean remains near the starting frequency, while the variance among replicates increases until alleles are fixed or lost.
lessons.workedExample: At p=0.5 and N=50, Var(p′)=0.25/100=0.0025 and SD≈0.05. At N=500 the SD is about 0.0158, showing weaker drift.
commonMistakes: Saying drift always lowers the frequency of rare alleles
Confusing expected change with the variance of change
Using census size automatically as effective size
lessons.keyStatement: Drift has no directional expectation for a neutral allele, but it creates variance among populations.
distractors: Drift is a deterministic force toward p=0.5.
Variance increases with N.
Neutral alleles cannot change frequency.
trueFacts: Drift is stronger in smaller populations.
Variance is largest near intermediate allele frequency.
Replicate populations diverge by chance.
falseFacts: The expected neutral change is always negative.
Fixation and loss are impossible under drift.

lessons.moduleDescription: Random sampling, fixation and loss, heterozygosity decay, and effective population size.
lessons.title: Fixation, loss, and absorption
lessons.summary: Calculate eventual fixation and loss probabilities for neutral alleles and distinguish probability from waiting time.
objectives: Relate initial copy number to fixation chance
sections: Absorbing boundaries
Once a neutral allele reaches p=0 it is lost; once it reaches p=1 it is fixed. These boundaries are absorbing in a model without mutation or migration.
Initial frequency determines the neutral probability
For a neutral allele, eventual fixation probability equals its current frequency. A new mutation present in one copy in a diploid population begins at 1/(2N), so most new neutral mutations are lost.
Probability is not speed

A larger population lowers the initial fixation probability of a single new mutation but also lengthens the stochastic trajectory. Fixation probability and expected time to absorption answer different questions.
variables: 1/(2N): initial frequency of one new copy in a diploid population
lessons.workedExample: A neutral allele present in 8 of 40 gene copies has p0=0.20, so its eventual fixation probability is 0.20 and loss probability is 0.80.
commonMistakes: Assuming a rare neutral allele cannot fix
Confusing eventual probability with next-generation probability
Using 1/N instead of 1/(2N) for one copy in a diploid population
lessons.keyStatement: For a neutral allele, fixation probability equals initial frequency.
distractors: Every neutral mutation has fixation probability 0.5.
Fixation probability equals selection coefficient.
A rare allele is guaranteed to disappear immediately.
trueFacts: Fixation and loss are absorbing states in the basic drift model.
A neutral allele at p=0.1 fixes with probability 0.1.
One new diploid copy starts at 1/(2N).
falseFacts: Probability of fixation specifies the exact fixation time.
Larger populations guarantee fixation.

lessons.moduleDescription: Random sampling, fixation and loss, heterozygosity decay, and effective population size.
lessons.title: Loss of heterozygosity and effective population size
lessons.summary: Use heterozygosity decay to connect drift intensity with effective population size and explain why Ne can differ from census size.
objectives: Calculate expected heterozygosity after t generations
Identify demographic causes of reduced Ne
sections: Identity by descent accumulates under drift
In each generation, two sampled gene copies have probability 1/(2Ne) of tracing to the same parental copy. Consequently, expected heterozygosity is multiplied by 1-1/(2Ne) per generation.
Long-term decay
After t generations, Ht=H0(1-1/(2Ne))^t. Smaller Ne produces faster loss of variation. The equation describes an expectation over replicate populations rather than the exact trajectory of one locus.
Why Ne differs from census size

Unequal reproductive success, skewed sex ratio, fluctuating population size, overlapping generations, and population structure can change the strength of drift. Ne is the size of an idealized population experiencing the same drift, not simply the number counted in the field.
variables: Ht: expected heterozygosity after t generations
lessons.workedExample: With H0=0.5, Ne=100 and t=50, Ht≈0.5(0.995)^50≈0.389. With Ne=1000, expected loss is much slower.
commonMistakes: Replacing Ne with census size without justification
Interpreting the expectation as an exact locus trajectory
Ignoring temporal fluctuation in population size
lessons.keyStatement: Effective population size calibrates the strength of drift in a real population.
distractors: Ne is always larger than census size.
Heterozygosity increases under pure drift.
All demographic processes affect Ne identically.
trueFacts: Smaller Ne accelerates heterozygosity loss.
Ne is defined relative to an idealized model.
Unequal family size can reduce Ne.
falseFacts: Ht must equal zero after Ne generations.
The equation gives the exact result for every locus.

lessons.title: The Wright–Fisher transition model
lessons.summary: Use the binomial transition probability to calculate the distribution of allele copies in the next generation.
sections: Model assumptions

The classical diploid Wright–Fisher model assumes fixed population size, non-overlapping generations, random union of gametes, and independent sampling of 2N gene copies. Neutrality is added when all copies have equal reproductive probability.
One-generation transition

If allele A has frequency p, the number j of A copies in the next generation follows a binomial distribution with 2N trials and success probability p. This gives the complete probability distribution, not only its mean and variance.
Markov property
The next generation depends on the present allele count, not on the full earlier history. Repeated application of the transition produces drift trajectories and eventual absorption.
lessons.workedExample: For N=2 and p=0.5, the probability of exactly two A copies next generation is C(4,2)(0.5)^4=6/16=0.375.

lessons.title: The Moran birth–death model
lessons.summary: Contrast one-at-a-time replacement with Wright–Fisher generational replacement and understand the resulting time scale.
objectives: Calculate upward and downward transition probabilities
Compare Moran and Wright–Fisher time scales
sections: One birth and one death per event
In the neutral Moran model, one individual is chosen to reproduce and one is chosen to die. Population size remains constant while allele count changes by at most one copy per event.
Transition probabilities

With i copies among N haploid individuals, the probability of increasing by one is (i/N)((N-i)/N), and the probability of decreasing by one is the same under neutrality. The remaining probability leaves the count unchanged.
Time-scale comparison

A Wright–Fisher generation replaces the whole population, whereas a Moran event replaces one individual. Comparing rates requires explicit conversion between events and generations; otherwise expected times appear inconsistent.
variables: N: haploid population size in the displayed form
lessons.workedExample: For i=2 and N=5, upward and downward probabilities are (2/5)(3/5)=0.24 each; the probability of no change is 0.52.

lessons.moduleDescription: Discrete- and continuous-generation models of stochastic allele-frequency change.
lessons.title: Wright–Fisher, Moran, and model choice
lessons.summary: Choose a stochastic model by matching biological life history and sampling scale rather than by convenience.
objectives: Justify model choice for a biological system
sections: Shared stochastic core

Both models describe random reproduction in finite populations and produce fixation, loss, and stronger drift at smaller population size. Their neutral fixation probability is determined by initial frequency.
Different biological approximations

Wright–Fisher is natural for synchronized, non-overlapping generations; Moran is useful for continuous turnover. Neither is a literal representation of every organism, and both can be modified for selection, mutation, migration, or variable size.
Model adequacy

The relevant question is whether the model captures the process needed for inference. A model can be useful despite unrealistic details if its assumptions are stated and sensitivity to violated assumptions is evaluated.

variables: model adequacy: correspondence between assumptions and question

lessons.title: Population subdivision and the Wahlund effect
lessons.summary: Explain why pooling differentiated populations creates heterozygote deficiency even when each subpopulation is in HWE.
objectives: Compare within- and pooled expected heterozygosity
sections: Local equilibrium, pooled disequilibrium

Each deme may satisfy HWE at its own allele frequency. Pooling individuals combines different p values, so the pooled expectation 2pbarqbar exceeds the average within-deme heterozygosity when allele frequencies differ.
Variance among demes
The heterozygote deficit is generated by variance in allele frequency among subpopulations. It is therefore a signal of unmodelled structure, not necessarily inbreeding within demes.
Practical consequence
Sample labels, geography, relatedness, and clustering must be inspected before HWE-based filtering or association analysis. Pooling can alter allele frequencies and create misleading departures.
variables: p_k: allele frequency in deme k
lessons.workedExample: Two equal demes have p1=0.8 and p2=0.2. pbar=0.5, HT=0.5, but HS=(0.32+0.32)/2=0.32, producing pooled heterozygote deficiency.

Ignoring unequal sample sizes

lessons.title: FST as a measure of differentiation
lessons.summary: Calculate FST from within- and total-population heterozygosity and interpret it as relative differentiation.
sections: Partitioning diversity
FST compares the reduction in heterozygosity within subpopulations with the heterozygosity expected from the pooled allele frequency. It is a standardized measure of differentiation.
Dependence on within-population diversity
The same absolute allele-frequency difference can produce different FST values depending on total heterozygosity. Highly polymorphic markers can constrain the maximum attainable FST.
Process is not identified by the statistic
Drift, migration, selection, demographic history, and sampling can all influence FST. Genome-wide context and explicit models are required before attributing a value to one process.
variables: HT: expected heterozygosity from pooled allele frequency
HS: mean expected heterozygosity within populations
lessons.workedExample: If HT=0.50 and HS=0.40, FST=(0.10)/0.50=0.20. Twenty percent of the relevant heterozygosity deficit is associated with population subdivision under this formulation.

lessons.title: Migration, drift, and population structure
lessons.summary: Understand how gene flow homogenizes allele frequencies while drift differentiates populations, and why equilibrium approximations require caution.
objectives: Explain opposing effects of migration and drift
Interpret PCA and ancestry as patterns of covariance
sections: Migration changes allele frequencies
After migration, the recipient frequency is a weighted average of local and migrant allele frequencies. Gene flow reduces differences when migrants come from populations with different frequencies.
Equilibrium approximation

Under a simplified island model, FST is related to the product of effective size and migration. This relation assumes equilibrium, symmetric migration, equivalent demes, and neutrality; real populations often violate these assumptions.
Structure analyses

PCA summarizes major axes of genotype covariance, and ancestry models describe statistical clusters under their assumptions. Neither method alone supplies a historical cause; relatedness, LD, sampling geography, and K choice must be examined.
lessons.workedExample: If p_local=0.8, p_migrant=0.2 and m=0.1, p′=0.74. Migration moves the local frequency toward the migrant source.
commonMistakes: Converting FST to migration without checking equilibrium assumptions

lessons.title: Coalescence of two lineages
lessons.summary: Derive the geometric waiting-time expectation for two sampled gene copies in a diploid population.
sections: Backward-time sampling

Two lineages coalesce in the previous generation if they choose the same parental gene copy. In a diploid Wright–Fisher population there are 2Ne possible parental copies, so the per-generation probability is 1/(2Ne).
Geometric waiting time
With constant probability c=1/(2Ne), the waiting time is geometric with expectation 1/c=2Ne generations. Large Ne produces deeper expected pairwise genealogies.
From genealogy to diversity

Mutations accumulate along branches. Under the neutral infinite-sites model, expected pairwise diversity is proportional to 4Neμ for autosomal diploids, linking population size, mutation, and observed variation.
variables: T2: waiting time for two lineages
μ: mutation rate per generation per site
lessons.workedExample: With Ne=10,000, two lineages have coalescence probability 1/20,000 each generation and expected waiting time 20,000 generations.

lessons.title: Waiting times with k lineages
lessons.summary: Calculate the rate at which any pair among k lineages coalesces and explain why recent intervals are short and deep intervals long.
objectives: Explain tree shape and total height
sections: Many possible pairs
With k lineages there are C(k,2) possible pairs. In the standard approximation, the probability that some pair coalesces in a generation is C(k,2)/(2Ne).
Expected interval
The expected time spent with k lineages is 2Ne/C(k,2)=4Ne/[k(k-1)]. As k decreases, intervals lengthen sharply.
Most tree height is deep
The final 2→1 coalescent interval has expectation 2Ne and contributes a large fraction of total tree height. By contrast, many coalescences near the tips occur quickly when k is large.

lessons.title: Coalescent expectations for molecular diversity
lessons.summary: Connect theta, expected pairwise differences, segregating sites, and the genealogy of a sample.
objectives: Relate theta to Ne and mutation
sections: Population mutation parameter
For autosomal diploids, theta=4Neμ. It combines the rate at which mutation enters a population with the genealogical time available for mutations to accumulate.
Two estimators, different frequency weighting

Pi weights variants by pairwise frequency, while Watterson’s theta uses the number of segregating sites corrected by the harmonic sum. Under the standard neutral equilibrium model, both estimate the same theta.
Genealogical interpretation

Long external branches create many low-frequency variants; long internal branches create intermediate-frequency variants. Demography and selection alter genealogies and therefore alter the allele-frequency spectrum.
commonMistakes: Treating theta as Ne without specifying μ
Comparing S without correcting for sample size
Assuming disagreement among estimators uniquely identifies selection

ThetaW corrects S for sample size.
Genealogy shape affects the SFS.
falseFacts: Different theta estimators must always agree in finite data.
A negative Tajima D proves a sweep.

lessons.title: Allele-frequency change under viability selection
lessons.summary: Calculate allele frequency after selection from genotype fitnesses and identify the role of dominance.
objectives: Interpret additive, dominant, and recessive effects
sections: Relative fitness
Assign genotype fitnesses wAA, wAa and waa. Before selection, genotype frequencies are weighted by fitness; division by mean fitness renormalizes surviving contributions.
Allele-frequency recursion
The A frequency after selection is the contribution of AA plus half the contribution of Aa, divided by mean fitness. The direction and speed depend on p, q, s, and dominance.
Dominance changes visibility to selection

A beneficial recessive allele changes slowly when rare because most copies occur in heterozygotes. A deleterious recessive allele can persist because selection cannot efficiently remove copies hidden in heterozygotes.
variables: wAA, wAa, waa: relative genotype fitnesses

Ignoring dominance when an allele is rare

Selection strength depends on allele frequency.
falseFacts: p′ can be calculated without q.

lessons.title: Fixation probability under selection and drift
lessons.summary: Compare neutral and selected fixation probabilities and understand the importance of the compound parameter Ne s.
objectives: Compare neutral and beneficial fixation probabilities
Explain the role of Ne s
Distinguish deterministic tendency from stochastic outcome
sections: Selection changes probability, not certainty

A beneficial mutation has a higher chance of fixation than a neutral mutation, but early stochastic loss remains common. A deleterious mutation has a lower chance, yet finite populations allow occasional fixation.
Weak-selection approximation
For a new additive beneficial mutation in a large diploid population, fixation probability is approximately 2s under the standard approximation. This is compared with the neutral probability 1/(2N).
Drift versus selection
The product Ne s indicates whether selection is strong relative to drift. When |Ne s| is small, trajectories are drift-dominated; when it is large, deterministic selection has greater influence.

variables: N or Ne: population size scale
Ne s: relative strength of selection to drift
lessons.workedExample: For s=0.01, the approximation gives Pfix≈0.02. In N=10,000, a neutral new mutation has probability 1/20,000=0.00005.
commonMistakes: Interpreting 2s as certainty
Using the approximation for any dominance or population size
Ignoring early stochastic loss

lessons.title: Purifying selection and mutation–selection balance
lessons.summary: Derive equilibrium frequencies for deleterious alleles under recurrent mutation and selection.
objectives: Calculate equilibrium for recessive and nonrecessive alleles
sections: Opposing processes

Mutation continually introduces deleterious alleles while selection removes them. At equilibrium, introduction and removal balance, so a deleterious allele can remain segregating without being adaptive.
Recessive case

For a fully recessive deleterious allele with fitness reduction s in aa, the equilibrium frequency is approximately sqrt(mu/s). Selection is inefficient when the allele is rare because most copies occur in heterozygotes.
Dominant or additive exposure
When heterozygotes experience selection hs, the low-frequency approximation is qhat≈mu/(hs). Stronger exposure in heterozygotes lowers equilibrium frequency.
lessons.equation: q̂≈√(μ/s) for a fully recessive deleterious allele; q̂≈μ/(hs) when heterozygotes are selected
variables: μ: mutation rate to the deleterious allele
lessons.workedExample: If μ=10^-6 and s=0.01 for a recessive deleterious allele, qhat=sqrt(10^-4)=0.01.
commonMistakes: Calling persistence evidence of benefit
Using the recessive formula when h>0
Ignoring back mutation and demographic assumptions

Recessive deleterious alleles can be hidden in heterozygotes.
Equilibrium depends on mutation and selection.
falseFacts: Persistence implies adaptation.
The formula is unaffected by dominance.

lessons.title: The site-frequency spectrum as demographic evidence
lessons.summary: Read folded and unfolded SFS and connect excesses of rare or intermediate variants to genealogical change.
objectives: Link demographic change to genealogy and spectrum
sections: Counting variants by frequency
The SFS records how many segregating sites occur at each derived-allele count. When ancestral state is unknown, minor-allele counts form the folded SFS.
Population expansion

Rapid growth creates many recent lineages and long external branches, producing an excess of rare variants. A bottleneck removes lineages and can transiently alter both diversity and the frequency spectrum.
Non-unique signatures
Selection and demography can create similar SFS distortions. Genome-wide patterns, linked information, multiple populations, and explicit model comparison are needed for stronger inference.
lessons.equation: ξ_i = number of sites with derived count i;  E[ξ_i]∝θ/i under the standard neutral model
variables: ξi: number of sites in class i
lessons.workedExample: In a sample of 10 sequences, singletons are i=1 and variants at count 9 are high-frequency derived. Without an outgroup, both are folded to minor count 1.
commonMistakes: Calling every singleton sequencing error
Mixing folded and unfolded spectra
Assigning one demographic history from one summary

lessons.title: Tajima’s D as a contrast of diversity estimators
lessons.summary: Explain the numerator of Tajima’s D and interpret its sign without treating it as a unique process detector.
sections: Two estimators of theta

Pi emphasizes intermediate-frequency variants through pairwise differences, whereas thetaW depends on the number of segregating sites. Under the standard neutral equilibrium model their expectations are equal.
Meaning of the sign
Negative D occurs when thetaW exceeds pi, often reflecting an excess of rare variants. Positive D occurs when pi exceeds thetaW, reflecting relatively more intermediate-frequency variants.
Interpretation requires context

Expansion, purifying selection, and selective sweeps can produce negative values; contraction, structure, or balancing selection can produce positive values. Significance depends on the appropriate null distribution and data quality.
lessons.workedExample: If pi is substantially below thetaW, the numerator is negative. This indicates a skew toward rare variants but does not identify whether growth or selection caused it.

lessons.title: Demographic model fitting and uncertainty
lessons.summary: Translate a demographic scenario into parameters, compare model expectations with data, and report uncertainty and non-identifiability.
sections: From history to parameters
A model may include ancestral size, current size, split time, migration rates, and growth. Each parameter changes expected genealogies and the one- or two-dimensional SFS.
Likelihood-based comparison
Methods compare the observed SFS with expectations under candidate parameter values. The best-fitting model is best among the tested candidates, not necessarily the true history.
Uncertainty and identifiability

Different histories can produce similar spectra, especially with limited samples or linked SNPs treated as independent. Confidence intervals, replicate optimization, residual plots, and simulation-based checks are required.
lessons.equation: L(Θ|SFS) ∝ P(observed SFS | demographic parameters Θ)
variables: Theta vector: population sizes, times, migration rates
likelihood: compatibility of data with a parameterized model
residual: observed minus expected SFS entry
lessons.workedExample: Two models may fit similarly if an older severe bottleneck and a recent mild bottleneck produce comparable spectra. Additional populations or LD information may separate them.
commonMistakes: Calling the highest likelihood model true
Reporting point estimates without uncertainty
Ignoring linkage or ancestral-state error
lessons.keyStatement: Demographic inference compares explicit histories and must report uncertainty and alternative explanations.
distractors: The best likelihood proves historical truth.
More parameters always improve inference.
Linked SNPs are automatically independent.
trueFacts: Different parameter combinations can fit similarly.
Residuals help diagnose model failure.
Simulation can evaluate fitted models.
falseFacts: Confidence intervals are unnecessary for large SNP datasets.
A one-population SFS directly estimates migration.

lessons.title: Selective sweeps and linked neutral variation
lessons.summary: Explain how positive selection changes diversity, the SFS, and LD around a selected site.
sections: Hitchhiking

A rapidly increasing beneficial allele carries linked neutral variants with it. Lineages near the selected site trace through the successful background, reducing local genealogical depth and diversity.
Frequency-spectrum signature
After a completed sweep, new mutations accumulate on recent branches, creating an excess of rare variants. High-frequency derived alleles may also occur when ancestral variants hitchhike.
Role of recombination

Recombination uncouples neutral sites from the selected allele. The affected genomic region is wider when selection is strong and recombination is low, but demography and background selection can mimic parts of the signal.
lessons.equation: Approximate sweep scale depends on s/r and local recombination; no single summary is sufficient
variables: linked neutral variant: no direct fitness effect but associated with selected background
lessons.workedExample: A sweep in a low-recombination region is expected to affect a broader interval than the same sweep in a high-recombination region.
commonMistakes: Calling every low-diversity region a sweep
Ignoring recombination-rate variation
Using Tajima D alone as proof
lessons.keyStatement: Selection at one site can reshape neutral variation at linked sites.
distractors: A sweep increases local genealogical depth.
Recombination strengthens linkage to the target.
Low diversity uniquely identifies positive selection.
trueFacts: Hitchhiking affects linked neutral variants.
Recombination limits the genomic span of a sweep.

falseFacts: Every selected allele creates the same sweep width.

lessons.title: Genome-wide scans and empirical null distributions
lessons.summary: Design a genome scan that accounts for non-independence, heterogeneous recombination, and demographic background.
objectives: Use windows and LD context appropriately
Control false positives and biological overclaiming
sections: Relative outliers
Genome scans compare loci or windows with a genomic background or simulated neutral distribution. Outliers are candidates for further investigation, not automatically selected loci.
Non-independence

Linked SNPs share genealogies, so clusters of extreme markers may represent one event. Window size should reflect marker density, recombination, and LD, while sensitivity to alternative choices should be checked.
Confounding

Mutation rate, background selection, recombination, mapping quality, missingness, and demography create heterogeneous statistics. Multiple complementary summaries and functional evidence strengthen inference.
lessons.equation: Outlier status = statistic relative to a justified empirical or simulated null distribution
variables: window: genomic interval used for summary
false positive: neutral locus classified as selected
lessons.workedExample: A cluster of high FST SNPs inside one LD block is not equivalent to many independent signals. Gene mapping should respect the linked interval and uncertainty.
commonMistakes: Counting linked SNPs as independent evidence
Using an arbitrary window without sensitivity analysis
Equating outlier with adaptation
lessons.keyStatement: Genome-scan outliers are hypotheses requiring genomic and biological validation.
distractors: Every outlier is adaptive.
Linked markers are independent.
Window size cannot affect results.
trueFacts: Demography shapes genome-wide statistics.
LD can cluster extreme SNPs.
Complementary summaries improve interpretation.
falseFacts: A Manhattan peak identifies the causal mutation exactly.
Functional annotation alone proves selection.

lessons.title: Distinguishing selection from demographic history
lessons.summary: Integrate locus-specific and genome-wide evidence to separate local selective effects from shared demographic processes.
sections: Scale of effect

Demographic events affect genealogies across much of the genome, whereas selection often creates localized deviations around targets. However, linked selection can also be widespread and recombination makes the background heterogeneous.
Conditioning on history
A demographic model fitted to putatively neutral data can generate a null distribution for selection statistics. Failure to include structure, size change, or migration inflates false positives.
Triangulation

A persuasive candidate combines an unusual statistic, local genomic context, replication, functional plausibility, and a pattern inconsistent with the demographic null. The conclusion should remain proportional to the evidence.
lessons.equation: Inference = observed local pattern compared with genome-wide and model-based demographic expectations
variables: demographic null: expected distribution under fitted history
replication: recurrence across independent data or populations
lessons.workedExample: A locus with high FST is more persuasive when it also shows a localized diversity/LD pattern and remains extreme under simulations of the inferred population history.
commonMistakes: Fitting demography only to candidate regions
Calling genome-wide skew evidence for many independent sweeps
Ignoring uncertainty in the demographic model
lessons.keyStatement: Selection inference is strongest when localized evidence exceeds expectations under demographic history.
distractors: Demography affects every locus identically.
One statistic separates selection from history.
Functional annotation is a null model.
trueFacts: Demographic misspecification can inflate outliers.
Localized multi-statistic evidence is stronger than one extreme value.
Conclusions should reflect model uncertainty.
falseFacts: A fitted demographic model is certainly true.
Selection cannot affect many loci.

lessons.moduleTitle: Theory extension: Recombination and linkage disequilibrium
lessons.moduleDescription: A focused extension from Hahn on recombination, LD statistics, and the population recombination parameter.
lessons.title: Recombination, linkage disequilibrium, and the population recombination parameter
lessons.summary: Use haplotype frequencies to calculate D and r², explain LD decay, and relate recombination to genealogical non-independence.
sections: Haplotype departure from independence
For alleles A/a and B/b, D=fAB-pA pB. D is zero when haplotype frequency equals the product of marginal allele frequencies. Its range depends on allele frequencies, motivating standardized measures.
Squared correlation
r²=D²/[pAqa pBqb] measures correlation between loci and is widely used to summarize LD. High LD can arise from tight linkage, recent admixture, drift, selection, or demographic history.
Population recombination parameter

For diploid autosomal data, rho=4Ne r. Recombination breaks associations along genealogies, but the observed LD landscape also depends on mutation, sample design, and history. LD is therefore not a direct physical-distance ruler.
commonMistakes: Equating LD with physical linkage
Comparing raw D across different allele frequencies
Interpreting rho as recombination rate without Ne
lessons.keyStatement: LD measures statistical association, not simply physical distance.
distractors: D is standardized between zero and one.
High LD proves physical adjacency.
rho equals r.
trueFacts: r² depends on D and allele frequencies.
Admixture can create LD.
Recombination erodes associations.
falseFacts: Unlinked loci can never show LD.
LD identifies causal direction.

lessons.moduleTitle: Theory extension: Molecular tests of selection
lessons.moduleDescription: A focused extension from Hahn on divergence, polymorphism, direct selection, and linked selection.
lessons.title: Direct and linked molecular tests of selection
lessons.summary: Integrate dN/dS, polymorphism-to-divergence contrasts, the McDonald–Kreitman framework, and linked-selection statistics.
sections: Divergence-based evidence

dN/dS compares nonsynonymous and synonymous substitution rates. Values below one commonly indicate purifying selection across sites; values above one can indicate recurrent positive selection, but averaging across sites and time can mask heterogeneity.
Polymorphism and divergence

The McDonald–Kreitman framework compares nonsynonymous to synonymous ratios within species and between species. An excess of nonsynonymous divergence relative to polymorphism is consistent with adaptive substitution under its assumptions.
Direct versus linked evidence

Direct tests use consequences at the focal sequence, whereas linked-selection tests use diversity, SFS, or haplotype patterns around a target. Demography, slightly deleterious variants, recombination variation, and ancestral-state error must be considered.
lessons.equation: Neutral MK expectation: P_N/P_S = D_N/D_S;  α = 1 - (D_S P_N)/(D_N P_S)
variables: DN,DS: nonsynonymous and synonymous fixed differences
alpha: estimated fraction of nonsynonymous substitutions driven by positive selection under the simple estimator
lessons.workedExample: If PN=20, PS=80, DN=40 and DS=80, alpha=1-(80×20)/(40×80)=0.5. Interpretation requires checking slightly deleterious polymorphism and demographic assumptions.
commonMistakes: Reading dN/dS>1 at one gene as proof without uncertainty
Ignoring slightly deleterious nonsynonymous polymorphisms in MK
Combining linked and direct statistics as independent when they share data
lessons.keyStatement: Direct and linked tests interrogate different consequences of selection and should be interpreted jointly but cautiously.
distractors: dN/dS below one proves no positive selection at any site.
The MK test uses only polymorphism.
Alpha is a selection coefficient.
trueFacts: Synonymous sites often provide a reference category.
Slightly deleterious polymorphisms can bias MK inference.
Recombination affects linked-selection signals.
falseFacts: Every nonsynonymous substitution is adaptive.
Demography has no effect on polymorphism-based tests.
